title: Unrecognized Southeast Alaska Native Communities Recognition and Compensation Act
short_title: Unrecognized Southeast Alaska Native Communities Recognition and Compensation Act
stage_label: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute (Amended) by Unanimous Consent.
plain_summary: This bill helps five Alaska Native communities in Southeast Alaska that were left out of an earlier land deal. The communities are Haines, Ketchikan, Petersburg, Tenakee, and Wrangell. Each community can now form a special corporation and receive about 23,000 acres of federal land. The Native people in these communities will get shares in their new corporations while keeping their existing rights. These corporations can also create trusts to help with health, education, and preserving their Native culture.
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute (Amended) by Unanimous Consent.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Unrecognized Southeast Alaska Native Communities Recognition and Compensation Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Purpose
sections.text: The goal of this law is to fix a problem. Five Alaska Native communities in southeast Alaska were left out of an earlier law called the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act. These communities are Haines, Ketchikan, Petersburg, Tenakee, and Wrangell. This new law allows Alaska Natives enrolled in these five communities to form special business companies called Urban Corporations. It also allows them to receive land that they should have gotten under the earlier law.

sections.title: Section 3  Establishment of Additional Native Corporations
sections.text: This section changes an existing law by adding new rules. The Native residents of the Native Villages of Haines, Ketchikan, Petersburg, Tenakee, and Wrangell in Alaska can now organize themselves into Urban Corporations. These are special business companies that Alaska Natives can form. Creating these new corporations will not affect the land rights of any Native Corporation that was already established before this law was passed.

sections.title: Section 4  Shareholder Eligibility
sections.text: This section explains who can own shares in the new Urban Corporations. The Secretary of the Interior must enroll individual Natives into the Urban Corporation for their respective village. Each Native person who gets enrolled and who was already a shareholder in the Regional Corporation for Southeast Alaska will receive one hundred shares of Settlement Common Stock in their Urban Corporation. If a Native person received shares in the Regional Corporation through inheritance from someone who originally enrolled in one of these five villages, and that deceased person was not already a shareholder in a Village Corporation or Urban Corporation, then the inheriting person will get the same number of shares in the new Urban Corporation as they inherited from the deceased person. These new share arrangements will not change any previous or future land allocations to Regional Corporations.

sections.title: Section 5  Distribution Rights
sections.text: This section changes the rules about how corporate money and profits are shared. Native members of the five villages who become shareholders in an Urban Corporation will continue to be eligible to receive money distributions as at large shareholders of the Regional Corporation for Southeast Alaska. The law also states that this new act will not change the ratio for determining how revenue is distributed among Native Corporations. It will also not affect existing settlement agreements among Regional Corporations or Village Corporations.

sections.title: Section 6  Compensation
sections.text:
This section creates detailed rules for giving land to the five new Urban Corporations. Each Urban Corporation will receive approximately twenty three thousand and forty acres of federal land. The Secretary of the Interior must transfer the surface rights of this land to each Urban Corporation, while the underground mineral rights go to the Regional Corporation for Southeast Alaska. The Secretary should complete these land transfers within two years of when each Urban Corporation is officially formed, though this deadline can be extended by up to one year if needed for certain legal processes.

The federal government will withdraw these lands from being available for other uses like mining or leasing until the transfers are complete. The land given to each Urban Corporation must be treated the same as land given under existing law, which means it has the same rules and protections.

The transferred lands will have public easements reserved, which means the public may still have certain rights to use roads or paths. The government must publish notice before terminating any public easement. The lands must remain open for public uses like hunting, fishing, and recreation, though the Urban Corporations can impose reasonable restrictions for safety, to prevent conflicts between different uses, or to protect cultural or environmental resources.

Any existing special use permits for guiding or outfitting on the transferred lands will end when the land is transferred, but the Urban Corporation must give the permit holder authorization to continue their activities for the remaining term plus one additional ten year renewal period.

The Secretary of Agriculture must try to make agreements for shared use of roads and transportation facilities between the Urban Corporations and the Forest Service. These agreements should allow both parties and their designees to use each other's roads and facilities.

The transferred land will include all roads, trails, log transfer facilities, leases, and related structures. Any existing rights of way, easements, or agreements will continue unless they expire naturally or are renegotiated.

Each Urban Corporation may establish a settlement trust to promote health, education, and welfare of their community members and preserve Native heritage and culture. Money from these trusts must first support elderly people and children, then other enrolled members.
